project: Voice-Based Medication Support for Older Adults with Mild Visual Impairment

records.content: Prof. Carter, I sent over a first draft. I started with voice assistants for older adults and ended up centering it on medication reminders at home, but I think it may still be trying to do too much. Could you tell me honestly if the scope is manageable?

Mia, there is something good in here, but right now it is doing four projects at once. You have trust, adherence, accessibility, and acceptance all mixed together. Pick one problem. The strongest one, to me, is what happens when a reminder is missed or unclear and the user has to recover from that.
Yes, I can see that now. I kept broadening it instead of choosing. I'll cut it back and focus on the reminder-repair part.

project: Voice-Based Medication Support for Older Adults with Mild Visual Impairment

records.content: I've reworked it. f2.pdf is now about breakdown recovery after a missed or confusing reminder, and I dropped most of the general technology-acceptance material. I also cut the diary part and switched to scenario sessions, which feels more realistic.

This version is much clearer. The next problem is the method. You say you want to evaluate whether the design works, but I still don't know what a successful recovery actually means in your study. Tighten that up. And think about using Wizard-of-Oz rather than a fixed script. You'll learn more from the interaction that way.
Got it. I'll define the recovery outcomes properly and move the prototype plan to Wizard-of-Oz.

project: Voice-Based Medication Support for Older Adults with Mild Visual Impairment

records.content: I've attached the third version. In f3.pdf I pinned down the recovery measures, tightened the contribution around repair strategies, and wrote out the Wizard-of-Oz protocol more carefully.
This is in good shape now. The question, participants, and method finally line up. Just keep yourself from drifting back into big adherence claims later unless the data really justifies it.
